item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. It did kind of come off as some otherkin's self-insert fantasy, but strangely it had enough charm to stand on it's own. A taste of fantasy spectacle is a rare break from the usual grimness of the Foundation, and I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this might be an unpopular opinion, but I like the happier SCPs. It proves that, even in a world of darkness, there is light.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm conflicted. As a lifelong Tolkien fan, the idea of stepping on the Straight Way and journeying to Elvenhome is something out of a daydream. But it seems far too straightforward and… benign for something set in the Foundation Universe.

It kills me to say it, but I think it would be better if there were multiple Exploration Logs, with each one being *radically* different depending on the personal tastes of the -A (someone who loves Narnia sees talking animals on the walls and hears a lion's roar; someone who loves Peter Pan sees Neverland, etc.), and the implication that whatever it really is, it's not any of those things at the end.

Alternatively, the Foundation could be their classic, overcontrolling self and try to fuck with the Faeries, resulting in a horrifying, and classically Fae, response, in which the city becomes hostile.

As it is, it's too noblebright for the foundation. No vote though; I couldn't bring myself to downvote something out of Tolkien.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3093-B

Did you mean 3903?

Also, the runes aren't showing up. Maybe you could add them as an image?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. I just found this skip from a cross link in another article and I gotta say, this is criminally underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the runes depend on your device's Unicode support (they are all Unicode characters). They show up fine both on my computer (running the latest version of Windows 10) and my iPhone (running the latest version of iOS as well).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who has been more or less been into fantasy, I really enjoyed reading this a lot. It really filled my head with a lot of beautiful imagery, and I really wish to see someone do fanart of this SCP.

This is probably one of the most beautiful SCPs - mainlist or otherwise - on this site, and it proves that an SCP doesn't have to be either scary, full of Paranoia Fuel, or straight-out wacky to be good.

Upvoted with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The atmosphere you created here is… well, magical. One small thing: the runes aren't showing up on mobile, is there a fix for that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

You effectively capture that magical/moonlight/fairy feeling. The skipping stones was an excellent touch to drive that point home. I especially liked the contrast of magical fairy dust vs. cold hard science. I hope we see a tale on this one, or an exploration log… maybe the Foundation manages to send a few agents into the city by brutally subverting the magical fairy dust trope. "We managed to splice DNA from two SCP-3903-A subjects into two agents, and by transplanting the eyeballs, heart, and 3 kg of neural tissue from the subjects into the agents, our agents were able to successfully create SCP-3903-B."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems genuine and heartful (I presume), but it's not too interesting. You give us a concept and idea, and tease at what it could be, but you stop short of bothering to explore it at all and instead just prop it up with the unconventional take of it being connected to otherkin. I mean, the way you wrote it has already made it much more genuine than the other article we have on otherkin but at the same time that's really the only major draw to this article, and that doesn't really count as a draw.

I wanna know more about this place, why it manifests the way it does, get sucked into its world, and there's nothing there. This isn't the sort of "Oooh, I love what went into this and I have to know more", it's "Oh, that's it"?. I know that you have Carling there to try and give us more information, but that's not a substitute for showing. It was still written well and kind enough that I'm not gonna downvote; I just think you leave off way too much potential here to really earn an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll admit that I saw "otherkin community" and got ready to downvote, but I'm glad I held off. It did turn out to be a good deal warmer and fuzzier than I could normally handle, but the imagery was done well enough to bridge that divide, so you should be proud of this +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Carling: Dude, this is fairy magic. You can’t quantify it with little gizmos or explain it with theories.

Gromwell: (chuckles) You must be new here. Lead the way, and let me know if you see anything unusual.

This exchange would have cemented your upvote if the rest hadn't done it already. The Foundation can and will quantify every damn marigold they can find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I don't speak Gaelic and couldn't find anyone who could, so I was going by Google translate. My logic was that the Fey probably speak their own dialect, so if it was wrong it could be explained easily enough. I might still change it to your suggestion.

Regarding your MIB bullshitting claim, that's a head-canon thing. There are plenty of depictions of the Foundation committing much bigger coverups.

Glad you liked it though. Honestly wasn't sure which way this was going to go. While I'm here shout out to my Beta Readers Bear-run Doctor Cimmerian DrLycus Ihp Lazar Lyusternik Leah-Red Pecan, Surrey (sorry if I spelt that wrong), and also to docterzero and psul who I just found out posted on the forum earlier today, but I had not read their feedback prior to posting. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a couple of nitpicks with the article.

First, the disinformation campaign is Men-in-Black levels of bullshitting. Something like that would in all likelihood just draw more attention to the SCP, rather than deflect attention.

“Go raibh maith agat le haghaidh ag tabhairt uirthi baile”.

My Irish is rusty, but a better translation would be "Go raibh maith agat as í a thabhairt abhaile." Luckily, Irish is fluid enough that your translation can slide.

Other than that, I'm a complete sucker for articles inspired by my home country's myths. +1
